title: How to Get a 5 in AP Physics
author: Tklesson Team
content:
You have decided to take AP Physics. Whether you are enrolled in AP Physics 1, AP Physics 2, or one of the AP Physics C courses, you are facing a demanding combination of conceptual reasoning, mathematical application, and laboratory analysis. The AP Physics exams are consistently among the most challenging AP exams, with five rates that are lower than most other subjects. But the challenge is not insurmountable. The students who earn fives are not necessarily the ones with the highest IQs. They are the ones who understand how the exam works and prepare strategically. Here is what you need to know.

Know Which Physics Course You Are Taking

AP Physics is not one course. It is four distinct courses, and the preparation strategies differ among them. AP Physics 1 is algebra-based and covers mechanics: motion, forces, energy, momentum, rotation, and simple harmonic motion. It is the most commonly taken AP Physics course and has the lowest five rate, typically around five to eight percent. AP Physics 2 is also algebra-based and covers fluid mechanics, thermodynamics, electricity, magnetism, optics, and modern physics. It builds on Physics 1 but does not require it as a formal prerequisite. AP Physics C: Mechanics uses calculus and covers mechanics in greater depth. AP Physics C: Electricity and Magnetism also uses calculus and covers electricity and magnetism in greater depth. The Physics C courses have higher five rates, around twenty-five to thirty percent, largely because the students who take them are self-selected for strong math skills.

Know which course you are in and what the exam covers. The College Board publishes detailed course descriptions with the exact topics and their weightings. Read them. Do not rely on your teacher to cover everything in class. Some topics may be rushed or skipped. You are responsible for all of them.

Focus on Conceptual Understanding First

The most common reason students score poorly on AP Physics exams is that they treat physics as a math course. They memorize formulas and plug in numbers. This approach fails because AP Physics questions are not primarily computational. They are conceptual. A typical multiple-choice question does not ask you to calculate a value. It asks you to predict what will happen to a system when a variable changes, or to identify which fundamental principle explains a phenomenon, or to select the correct free-body diagram for a situation.

For every concept, you need to be able to answer these questions: What is the fundamental principle? What are the conditions under which it applies? What are the common misconceptions students have about it? What would happen if one variable changed? The students who earn fives can explain physics in words, not just in equations.

The free-response questions reinforce this emphasis. The AP Physics 1 and 2 exams include a section called Qualitative Quantitative Translation, where you must analyze a scenario both conceptually and mathematically. You must explain your reasoning in paragraphs, not just show calculations. The Physics C exams also require written justifications, though the emphasis on prose is slightly less.

Master the Laboratory and Experimental Design Questions

Every AP Physics exam includes questions about experimental design. You may be asked to describe a procedure for measuring a physical quantity, identify sources of experimental error, or explain how to reduce uncertainty. These questions are worth a significant portion of the free-response section, and many students are unprepared for them because their lab experience was limited or they treated lab as a separate activity from the rest of the course.

Review the basic principles of experimental design. How do you measure a quantity indirectly? How do you graph data to extract a physical relationship? What is the difference between systematic and random error? How do you choose appropriate equipment for a measurement? These are not obscure topics. They are the core of the experimental design questions that appear on every exam.

Practice writing clear experimental procedures. A good procedure is specific enough that someone else could follow it and obtain similar results. It identifies the equipment needed, the measurements to be taken, the variables to be controlled, and the analysis to be performed. Vague procedures earn partial credit at best.

Use Practice Questions to Diagnose Your Weaknesses

AP Physics questions are not interchangeable with textbook problems. Textbook problems tend to be computational and straightforward. AP Physics questions are often multi-step, conceptual, and require you to integrate multiple topics. Practice with real AP questions from released exams. The College Board makes past free-response questions available online. Use them.

When you miss a question, diagnose the error specifically. Did you misunderstand the physics? Did you apply the wrong principle? Did you make a math error? Did you misinterpret the diagram? Did you not understand what the question was asking? The more specific your diagnosis, the more targeted your remediation can be.

If you are in AP Physics 1 or 2, practice the types of questions that are unique to your exam. Physics 1 emphasizes rotational motion and angular momentum, topics that are challenging because they are less intuitive than linear motion. Physics 2 emphasizes magnetism and modern physics, topics that are abstract and require comfort with field concepts and quantum ideas. Identify the topics that are most heavily weighted on your specific exam and prioritize them.

The Bottom Line

A five on an AP Physics exam requires conceptual understanding, laboratory skills, and strategic practice. Know which physics course you are taking and what the exam covers. Focus on explaining physics in words, not just solving equations. Prepare for experimental design questions by reviewing measurement principles and practicing writing procedures. Use released AP questions to diagnose your weaknesses. The exam is difficult, but the skills it tests are learnable. Understand the physics, and the five is within reach.
